pattern: A cut's total cost is its price times how many pieces of the other dimension it has to pass through, so making the most expensive cuts first -- while the cake is still least subdivided in the perpendicular direction -- minimizes the total.
hints: Whenever you make a horizontal cut, it has to run through every piece the cake is currently divided into along the vertical direction (and symmetrically for a vertical cut) -- so a cut's cost is multiplied by how many perpendicular pieces already exist.

That multiplier only grows as more cuts happen, so an expensive cut should be made as early as possible, before the perpendicular count has grown -- process all cuts (both directions together) from the most expensive to the cheapest.

Track two counters starting at 1 (current number of horizontal and vertical pieces); processing cuts by descending cost, add cost times the *other* direction's current counter to the total, then increment that cut's own direction counter.

edge_inputs.description: single row, only vertical cuts needed
single column, only horizontal cuts needed
tied cut costs across both directions
one very expensive cut among many cheap ones

stress.description: random grid sizes with random cut costs
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 3000)))
    n = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 3000)))
    horizontalCut = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(m - 1)]
    verticalCut = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n - 1)]
    return [m, n, horizontalCut, verticalCut]
stress.note: sizes scale up toward the real constraint
